355	3. **`ChartAlgebra.lean`** — Scaffold the file with declarations for `thm:chart_id`, `thm:chart_comp`, `thm:chart_inv` from the chapter; leave bodies as `sorry`. Blueprint: `chapters/ChartAlgebra.tex`. (File-skeleton dispatch — see below.)
356	```
357	
358	**Agent count = file count.** The dispatcher fans out one prover per file. When a file has multiple ready sorries, list ALL of them under that file's objective — the prover handles them sequentially within one lane. Splitting a multi-sorry file across iters is artificial throttling; one lane working sequentially on three sorries finishes faster than waiting two iters for three single-sorry lanes (the prover keeps its file context warm across sorries).
359	
360	**File-skeleton dispatches.** When a blueprint chapter is complete but the corresponding `.lean` file does not yet exist (or exists but is missing declarations the chapter introduced), it is a legitimate iter objective to dispatch a prover with directive *"scaffold `Foo.lean` with declarations for `thm:a`, `thm:b`, `thm:c` from the chapter; leave bodies as `sorry`; add the import + namespace boilerplate; do not attempt to prove anything yet"*. The next iter then fills the sorries. This is materially faster than one-iter-per-declaration scaffolding.
361	
362	**Mechanical-vs-deep partition.** Sorries split into two regimes:
363	- *Mechanical* — typeclass wiring, instance synthesis, ring-level algebra, simp/ring-tactic territory, definitional unfolding glue. A prover lane can comfortably close 3–6 of these in one iter (the attempt-cap permits each one a fresh budget). When the upcoming work is mechanical, load lanes aggressively.
364	- *Deep* — the load-bearing categorical / geometric / analytic argument. One per lane, often less. The prover may also legitimately spend an iter exploring without closing anything.
365	
366	Use this partition to decide how thickly to load each lane. Don't load a deep lane with three deep sorries; that just thrashes the prover's attempt budget across all three. Don't restrict a mechanical lane to one sorry "to keep it simple"; the prover wants the batched objectives.
367	
368	Balance difficulty so all provers finish around the same time. Avoid shallow / trivial objectives unless they unblock something downstream this iter. Don't artificially throttle — the prover prompt says "push as far as possible"; your objective list must give it room to.
369	
370	If a previous experiment is being restarted, check compilation status of every target `.lean` first. Prioritize files with sorries or compile errors; don't redo completed work.
371	
372	**Dispatch cap.** The runner refuses to fan out more than ~10 provers in a single iter (configurable via `--max-objectives`, default 10). Writing 15+ files into `## Current Objectives` is a planning failure, not a tooling limitation — even when the project has many open files, the right iter-level move is to pick the most urgent ≤10 (mechanical lanes counted) and defer the rest to the next iter. If the deterministic plan-validate guard truncates your list, the surplus is added to `USER_HINTS.md` so the next planner sees what got deferred. Don't rely on the safety net — pick the right ~10 the first time.
373	
374	**Blocked-deps filter.** Before dispatch, plan-validate also drops any objective whose transitive local imports failed the *previous* `lake build`. Reason: a prover assigned to `Downstream.lean` that imports `Upstream.lean`-which-doesn't-compile would fail to even load the file, burning API time for nothing. The blocked set is parsed from `.archon/last_lake_build.log`. There is one important exception: a blocked file that's *itself* an objective this iter is presumed-being-fixed — the planner is allowed to assign `Upstream.lean` and `Downstream.lean` together (the prover phase handles them in import order). When the filter drops files, they're listed in `USER_HINTS.md` with their specific blocking deps, so you can prioritize fixing the upstream files next iter. Best practice: when you see `## Build state` flagging compile errors, put those files at the top of `## Current Objectives` so the filter exempts the downstream lanes that depend on them.
375	
376	## Dependency graph
377	
378	Optional but cheap. Before scoping objectives, you may run:
379	
380	```
381	${LEAN4_PYTHON_BIN:-python3} "$LEAN4_SCRIPTS/dependency_graph.py" . --format=summary
382	```
383	
384	It parses imports + chapter `\lean{...}` / `\uses{...}` / `\proves{...}` / markers and emits a project-wide view in under a second. Use it to order objectives — upstream files first, downstream files later.
385

389	
390	- `autoformalize` → `prover` (all statements formalized)
391	- `prover` → `polish` (all sorries filled and verified)
392	- `polish` → `COMPLETE` (proofs clean, compile)

1	# Strategy
2	
3	## Goal
4	
5	Formalize Christian Merten's Jacobian challenge (`references/challenge.lean`):
6	nine protected declarations headlined by `AlgebraicGeometry.Jacobian` and
7	`Jacobian.nonempty_jacobianWitness` — existence of an Albanese / Jacobian
8	object uniform over the `k`-rational pointing of a smooth proper
9	geometrically irreducible curve `C / k`, with **no** `C(k) ≠ ∅` hypothesis.
10	End-state: zero inline `sorry`, kernel-only axioms (`propext`,
11	`Classical.choice`, `Quot.sound`). **Char-general:** the protected
12	signatures take `[Field k]` only — NO `CharZero`.
13	
14	The witness OBJECT `J` is always real (constructed unconditionally); only
15	`isAlbaneseFor` is universally quantified over `P : 𝟙_ _ ⟶ C`. Spine:
16	**pointed vs. unpointed**. `genus C := \dim_k H^1(C, O_C)` (arithmetic
17	genus; protected — cannot be re-typed).
18	
19	## Phases & estimations

47	| **Genus-0 RR.2.H¹ — skyscraper-flasque vanishing (project-side)** | committed; chapter `RR_H1Vanishing` drafting | ~8–12 | ~200–400 · gated | flasque sheaves; H¹ vanishing of flasque on noetherian | NEW (H¹ on critical path via genus def) |
48	| **Genus-0 RR.2 assembly — χ-additivity + LES** | bundles H⁰ + H¹ halves | ~4–8 | ~150–300 · gated | LES-of-Ext; 6-term alternating-rank identity | gated on RR.2.H¹ |
49	| **Genus-0 RR.3 — `O_C(P)` global sections** | 4 of 7 sorries axiom-clean; CONVERGING | ~7–15 | ~150–350 · ~30/it | invertible sheaf at point; restriction sequence | downstream sorries gated on RR.2.H¹ |
50	| **Genus-0 RR.4 — rational ⟹ `≅ ℙ¹`** | `Hom.poleDivisor` refactored to positive-part route; Pin 2 closure via positivePart substrate | ~5–9 | ~250–450 · gated | `Proj.fromOfGlobalSections`; degree-1 iso; positivePart degree identity | finishes the bridge |
51	| **`genusZeroWitness` body + `k̄→k` descent** | gated on rigidity + RR bridge | ~7–10 | ~350–850 · gated | terminal cluster on Spec k; faithfully-flat descent | descent LOCKED Spec-k-direct |
52	| **`nonempty_jacobianWitness` body** | gated on both arms | 1 | <50 · gated | `by_cases h : genus C = 0` | trivial once both arms close |
53	
54	**Total positive-genus arm (Route A)**: ~290–520 iters / ~9200–16400 LOC.
55	**Total genus-0 arm**: ~45–65 iters / ~2200–3800 LOC.
56	
57	## Routes
58	
59	Positive-genus arm = **Route A (Picard scheme via FGA)** — mandatory.
60	Genus-0 arm = **Route C (Milne §I.3 rigidity)** — `J = Spec k` trivial,
61	char-general.
62	
63	### Route A — Picard scheme via FGA
64	
65	`J := Pic⁰_{C/k}` per Kleiman §4–§5 + Nitsure §5 + Milne III §6. A.2 +
66	A.3 + A.4 decomposed per table. A.4.a = dominant risk; A.4.b
67	independently startable.
68	
69	Dependency graph: A.1.b ⊳ A.1.a; A.1.c ⊳ A.1.b; A.2.a.ii ⊳ A.2.a.i;
70	A.2.a.iii ⊳ A.2.a.ii; A.2.b.ii ⊳ A.1.a + A.2.a.i; A.2.b.iii ⊳ A.2.b.i +
71	A.2.b.ii; A.2.c ⊳ A.2.b.iii + A.1.c; A.3.0 parallel; A.3.i parallel;
72	A.3.ii ⊳ A.2.c + A.3.i; A.3.iii ⊳ A.3.0 + A.3.ii; A.3.iv ⊳ A.3.0 +
73	A.3.iii; A.3.v ⊳ A.3.ii (parallel with iii/iv); A.3.vi ⊳ A.3.ii
74	(parallel with iii/iv); A.3.vii ⊳ A.3.ii; A.4.c ⊳ A.4.a + A.4.b;

78	### Route C — genus-0 rigidity via Milne §I.3
79	
80	`J = Spec k` trivial. Over `k̄`, every pointed `f : C → A` is constant.
81	Rigidity Lemma + Cor 1.5 + Cor 1.2 axiom-clean. Base case `ℙ¹→A const`
82	via 𝔾_m-scaling shortcut (chart-bridge III.c separated-locus). Genus-0
83	⟹ ℙ¹ via RR.1–RR.4 bridge; RR.2 H¹ skyscraper vanishing committed as
84	project-side build; Lane I Pin 2 closes via positivePart Weil-divisor
85	substrate.

87	## Open strategic questions
88	
89	- **End-state contract**: zero inline `sorry`, kernel-only axioms
90	  (`propext`, `Classical.choice`, `Quot.sound`). Every named typed
91	  sorry must reduce to either a Mathlib upstream or a project-side
92	  substrate with an explicit iter budget — no permanent named sorries.
93	- **`k̄→k` descent — LOCKED to Spec-k-direct.** Genus-0 witness body

100	  upstream PR landing.

108	- **REJECTED — `Sym^g` AS THE JACOBIAN OBJECT**: requires k-rational

110	- **REJECTED — `Pic⁰`-functor-of-points Yoneda UP**: route shifts the

57	## CRITICAL — integration build RED
58	
59	The iter-190 paired Lane I prover dispatch split a 2-file work item
60	("public `positivePart` def in `WeilDivisor.lean` + consume in
61	`RationalCurveIso.lean`") across two parallel prover sessions. Each
62	session built its own snapshot. The `WeilDivisor.lean` session landed
63	the public `Scheme.WeilDivisor.positivePart` def per spec; the
64	`RationalCurveIso.lean` session, not seeing the public def in its
65	snapshot, landed its planned file-local fallback `private
66	noncomputable def WeilDivisor.positivePart` at L416. At integration,
67	Lean rejects:
68	
69	```
70	error: AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RationalCurveIso.lean:416:26:
71	  a non-private declaration `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.positivePart`
72	  has already been declared
73	```
74	
75	The `private` keyword does NOT shadow the imported public name —
76	Lean's namespace resolution is at the fully-qualified level. The
77	iter-190 plan's instruction "the Pin 2 body close lands in
78	RationalCurveIso.lean **after the dependency lands**" implied
79	serialisation but was dispatched in parallel.
80	
81	**This is the single most consequential finding of iter-190 review.**
82	Until iter-191 resolves it, no downstream prover lane can be sanely
83	dispatched (every `lake build` returns 1; sync_leanok runs only because
84	its per-file mode is partly resilient to the downstream break).
85	
86	Recommendations.md §1 lays out the concrete fix recipe (~50 LOC edit
87	in RationalCurveIso.lean) and §2 lays out the prevention rule
88	(serialise paired prover dispatches across iters, OR mandate
89	distinctive private names for parallel fallbacks).

25	### Attempt 1

27	  `Finsupp.semilatticeSup` exists and `D ⊔ 0` typechecks on `α →₀ ℤ` but is

30	- **Dead end**: `D ⊔ 0` form works but obscures the underlying `mapRange` for downstream lemmas (e.g. `positivePart_zero`); going via `Finsupp.mapRange` is equivalent and more concrete.

34	  is the canonical Mathlib packaging that maps coefficient-wise through a
35	  zero-preserving function (the `by simp` discharges `0 ⊔ 0 = 0`).
36	- **Result**: RESOLVED, axiom-clean.

42	## `Scheme.WeilDivisor.positivePart_zero` (L507)
43	
44	### Attempt 1
45	- **Approach**: Direct via `Finsupp.mapRange_zero`.
46	- **Tooling trap encountered**: `X.WeilDivisor` is a `def` (not abbrev / not
47	  reducible), so Lean's elaborator does NOT see `positivePart D` as a
48	  `Finsupp` for function-application. The Coordinate-wise unfold lemma
49	  `positivePart_apply : (positivePart D) Y = D Y ⊔ 0` requires fighting this:
50	  ascriptions like `(positivePart D : X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ)` fail because
51	  there's no registered coercion from `X.WeilDivisor` to `X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ`
52	  (the def itself is the type identity, but elaboration doesn't unfold it).
53	- **Resolution**: Use `change Finsupp.mapRange ... = ...` at the statement
54	  level inside the proof block. The `change` tactic does unfold the def-
55	  equal type at the goal level, then `Finsupp.mapRange_zero` closes by `exact`.
56	- **Result**: RESOLVED, axiom-clean.
57	
58	### Dropped: `positivePart_apply` lemma
59	
60	The natural coord-wise unfold lemma
61	```
62	lemma positivePart_apply (D : X.WeilDivisor) (Y : X.PrimeDivisor) :
63	    positivePart D Y = D Y ⊔ 0
64	```
65	does NOT elaborate because `positivePart D` has type `X.WeilDivisor` (a
66	`def`), and Lean does not see this as a `Finsupp` for function-application.
67	Workarounds tried (ascription, `show`, `change`) all failed to make the
68	*statement* itself elaborate (they only help inside proof bodies).
69	
70	**Recommendation for iter-191+**: either (a) refactor `Scheme.WeilDivisor`
71	from `def` to `abbrev` (cross-file impact, ~all consumers need a re-typecheck),
72	or (b) leave the apply-lemma off and have callers use `change` inside their
73	proofs. Option (b) is what `Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal_degree_zero` (L444)
74	already does via `apply Finsupp.ext; intro Y; change ... = 0` — consistent
75	project-side idiom.

470	
471	/-- **The pole divisor of a morphism `φ : C → ℙ¹` of smooth proper curves
472	over `k̄`.** As a Weil divisor on `C`, it is `φ^*[∞]` — the pullback of the
473	divisor `[∞]` on `ℙ¹` along `φ`.
474	
475	**iter-190 substantive body (Pin 2 corrective Option (a) — positive part of
476	principal).** The body is the *positive part* of the principal Weil divisor
477	on `C` of the pulled-back local parameter
478	`algebraMap K(ℙ¹) K(C) (localParameterAtInfty kbar).val`:
479	
480	  `Hom.poleDivisor φ := positivePart (principal (algebraMap K(ℙ¹) K(C) t_∞) _)`
481	
482	Mathematically, `div(φ^* t_∞) = (φ^* t_∞)_0 − (φ^* t_∞)_∞` on `C`, where
483	`(φ^* t_∞)_0 = φ^*[∞]` (the zeros of `φ^* t_∞` are exactly the preimages
484	of `∞`, where `t_∞` vanishes on `ℙ¹`) and `(φ^* t_∞)_∞ = φ^*[0]` (the
485	poles are the preimages of `0`). Extracting the positive part of
486	`div(φ^* t_∞)` exactly recovers `φ^*[∞]` on the nose.
487	
488	**Why the positive-part wrap is essential (Pin 2 structural-conflict
489	diagnosis, iter-189 → iter-190 corrective).** The naked principal divisor
490	`principal (algebraMap _ _ t_∞) halg` has degree zero on a complete
491	nonsingular curve (Hartshorne II.6.10 / Stacks 0BE3,
492	`Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal_degree_zero` of `WeilDivisor.lean`),
493	whereas the RHS `Module.finrank K(ℙ¹) K(C)` of
494	`Hom.poleDivisor_degree_eq_finrank` is positive (= deg φ ≥ 1) at every
495	call site that uses the φ-induced algebra structure. The positive-part

527	    [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]
528	    [IsLocallyNoetherian C.left]

530	    [IsIntegral (ProjectiveLineBar kbar).left]

533	    C.left.WeilDivisor :=
534	  -- Pull a chosen local parameter `t_∞ ∈ K(ℙ¹)` back to `K(C)` along the

536	  -- extract the positive part (i.e. the zeros, which on a complete curve

539	  -- `K(ℙ¹) → K(C)` (a ring hom out of a field is injective).
540	  let t := localParameterAtInfty kbar
541	  have halg :
542	      algebraMap (ProjectiveLineBar kbar).left.functionField
543	          C.left.functionField t.val ≠ 0 := by
544	    intro h
545	    apply t.property
546	    have hinj :
547	        Function.Injective

553	            C.left.functionField 0 = 0 :=
554	      RingHom.map_zero _

558	      (algebraMap (ProjectiveLineBar kbar).left.functionField

626	/-- **Degree of a non-constant morphism to `ℙ¹` via its pole divisor**
627	(Hartshorne IV §2 p. 299 opening + II §6.9 multiplicativity of degree
628	under finite pullback).
629	
630	For a non-constant morphism `φ : C ⟶ ProjectiveLineBar k̄` from a smooth
631	proper geometrically irreducible curve `C` over an algebraically closed
632	field `k̄`, there exists a positive integer `d` (= `deg(φ)`) together
633	with a Weil divisor `D` on `C` (= the pole divisor `φ^*[∞]` of the
634	pulled-back affine coordinate) of total degree `d`.
635	
636	The non-constancy hypothesis is encoded as `∀ Q, φ ≠ toUnit C ≫ Q`
637	(no constant morphism `C ⟶ ℙ¹` agrees with `φ`); this is equivalent to
638	`φ` being surjective onto `ℙ¹_{k̄}` since the image is a closed
639	irreducible subset of `ℙ¹_{k̄}` of dimension ≥ 1, and the only such is
640	`ℙ¹_{k̄}` itself.
641	
642	iter-178+ body: a non-constant morphism between smooth proper curves
643	over `k̄` is automatically finite (proper + quasi-finite). The
644	function-field extension `k̄(ℙ¹) ↪ K(C)` is finite of some degree
645	`d = [K(C) : k̄(ℙ¹)] ≥ 1`. Take `D := φ^*[∞] ∈ Div(C)` via the standard
646	"pullback of a Weil divisor along a finite morphism" operation; by
647	Hartshorne II.6.9, `deg(D) = deg(φ) · deg([∞]) = d · 1 = d`. The
648	positivity `d ≥ 1` follows from non-constancy (else `deg(φ) = 0` would
649	force `φ` constant). Both the finite-pullback operation and the
650	multiplicativity-of-degree identity are project-bespoke sub-lemmas
651	threaded inside the iter-178+ body (cf. the closely related
652	`Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal_degree_zero` of `RR.1`).

515	For a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve `C` over an algebraically
516	closed field `kbar`, and a finite extension `K → K(C)` of function fields
517	(arising as the pullback along a non-constant morphism `C → ℙ¹` of the
518	function field `K(ℙ¹)`), there exists a non-zero element `t ∈ K` (a local
519	parameter at `∞ ∈ ℙ¹`) such that the degree of the positive part of the
520	principal divisor of `algebraMap K K(C) t` equals the function-field
521	extension degree:
522	\[
523	  \deg\bigl((\mathrm{div}\,(\varphi^{\#}\,t))_0\bigr)
524	  \;=\; [K(C) : K] \;=\; \mathrm{Module.finrank}_{K} K(C).
525	\]
526	Equivalently, `(div (φ^# t))_0 = φ^*[∞]` and `deg(φ^*[∞]) = deg(φ)`
527	(Hartshorne II.6.9 specialised at `D = [∞]`).

529	iter-190 plan-phase typed-sorry pin: this is the public face of the

537	arbitrary trap that a naked equation form would carry (the equation is
538	true only when `t` is a uniformiser at a closed point of `ℙ¹`; the
539	existential witness is the local parameter at `∞`).

557	/-! ## §7. Linear equivalence and the divisor class group -/
558	
559	/-- **Linear equivalence of Weil divisors.**
560	
561	Two Weil divisors `D, D' ∈ Div(X)` are linearly equivalent, written `D ~ D'`, if
562	and only if there exists a nonzero rational function `f ∈ K(X)^×` with
563	`D - D' = div(f) ∈ Div(X)`.
564	
565	`~` is an equivalence relation (reflexivity from `div(1) = 0`, symmetry from
566	`div(f⁻¹) = -div(f)`, transitivity from `div(fg) = div(f) + div(g)`, all via
567	`thm:principal_hom`). The quotient `Cl(X) := Div(X) / im(div)` is the
568	**divisor class group** of `X`.
569	
570	On a smooth proper curve `C` over `k̄`, `thm:principal_deg_zero` shows the
571	degree map descends to `deg : Cl(C) → ℤ`.
572	
573	Blueprint reference: `def:linear_equivalence` (Hartshorne II §6 p. 131). -/
574	def LinearEquivalence [IsIntegral X] [IsLocallyNoetherian X]
575	    [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne X] (D D' : X.WeilDivisor) : Prop :=
576	  ∃ (f : X.functionField) (hf : f ≠ 0), D - D' = principal f hf
577	
578	end Scheme.WeilDivisor
579	
580	end AlgebraicGeometry
581

1	# AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/AuslanderBuchsbaum.lean (Lane G, iter-190)
2	
3	## Summary
4	
5	**HARD BAR met**: 2 axiom-clean intermediate helpers landed for the
6	Stacks 00NQ project-side build:
7	
8	1. `isDomain_of_isLocalRing_of_spanFinrank_maximalIdeal_eq_zero` — base case.
9	2. `regularLocal_quotient_isRegularLocal_of_notMemSq` — inductive step prep
10	   (R/(x) regular local of `spanFinrank = k` without needing `IsSMulRegular`).
11	
12	The main `isDomain_of_regularLocal` body was refactored from a bare `sorry`
13	into a structured strong induction on `spanFinrank 𝔪 R`:
14	
15	- **Base case** (`n = 0`): closed via the new base-case helper.
16	- **Inductive step** (`n = k + 1`):
17	  - Pick `x ∈ 𝔪 \ 𝔪²` (axiom-clean Nakayama witness).
18	  - Build R/(x) regular local of `spanFinrank = k` (axiom-clean helper).
19	  - IH gives R/(x) IsDomain, so (x) is prime.
20	  - Pick minimal prime `𝔭 ≤ (x)`.
21	  - **Case `x ∉ 𝔭`** (axiom-clean closure):
22	    - `𝔭 ⊆ jacobson R • 𝔭` via the `y = x·z, z ∈ 𝔭` argument.
23	    - Nakayama (`Submodule.FG.eq_bot_of_le_jacobson_smul`) → `𝔭 = ⊥`.
24	    - `(⊥).IsPrime` → `IsDomain R` via `IsDomain.of_bot_isPrime`.
25	  - **Case `x ∈ 𝔭`** (focused typed sorry, iter-191+ target):
26	    - `𝔭 = (x)` is a minimal prime of R.
27	    - Requires either the graded-ring isomorphism
28	      `gr_𝔪(R) ≅ κ[X₁,…,X_d]` (Stacks `lemma-regular-graded`, NOT in
29	      Mathlib at pinned commit `b80f227`) or an alternative bypass.
30	
31	## Sorry count
32	
33	- **Before**: 2 sorries (`auslander_buchsbaum_formula` L835,
34	  `isDomain_of_regularLocal` L1300 bare).
35	- **After**: 2 sorries (`auslander_buchsbaum_formula` L843 unchanged,
36	  `isDomain_of_regularLocal` L1537 focused on the `x ∈ 𝔭` branch).
37	- **Net**: 0 sorries gained or closed, but substrate dramatically narrowed:
38	  the residual sorry is now scoped to a single explicit case (`x ∈ 𝔭`
39	  ↔ `(x) ∈ minimalPrimes R`), with the algebraic core of Stacks 00NQ
40	  (Krull intersection + Nakayama, in the form `𝔭 ⊆ 𝔪·𝔭 → 𝔭 = ⊥`) closed
41	  **axiom-clean** in the `x ∉ 𝔭` branch.
42	
43	## Lemmas added (axiom-clean, iter-190 Lane G)
44	
45	### `isDomain_of_isLocalRing_of_spanFinrank_maximalIdeal_eq_zero` (L1299)
46	
47	Stacks 00NQ base case. `spanFinrank 𝔪 R = 0` ⟹ `𝔪 = ⊥` ⟹ R is a field
48	⟹ R is a domain.
49	
50	- `Submodule.spanFinrank_eq_zero_iff_eq_bot` for the FG case.
51	- `IsLocalRing.isField_iff_maximalIdeal_eq` for the field characterisation.
52	- `IsField.isDomain` for the final step.
53	
54	### `regularLocal_quotient_isRegularLocal_of_notMemSq` (L1323)
55	
56	Stacks 00NU axiom-clean. For `R` regular local of `spanFinrank 𝔪 = k + 1`
57	and `x ∈ 𝔪 \ 𝔪²`, the quotient `R / (x)` is regular local of
58	`spanFinrank 𝔪' = k`.
59	
60	- Cotangent dim drop via `finrank_cotangentSpace_quot_span_singleton_succ`
61	  (existing iter-188 helper).
62	- `IsLocalRing.spanFinrank_maximalIdeal_eq_finrank_cotangentSpace` to
63	  translate finrank ↔ spanFinrank.
64	- Dim lower bound via `ringKrullDim_le_ringKrullDim_quotient_add_encard`
65	  (Krull's height theorem — does **NOT** require `x` to be a non-zero-divisor;
66	  this is the key axiom-clean advantage over
67	  `ringKrullDim_quotient_span_singleton_succ_eq_ringKrullDim`).
68	- Dim equation extraction via `WithBot.add_eq_coe` + `WithTop.add_right_cancel`
69	  (matching the existing pattern in
70	  `exists_isSMulRegular_quotient_isRegularLocal_succ`).
71	- `IsRegularLocalRing.of_spanFinrank_maximalIdeal_le` for the final step.
72	
73	## isDomain_of_regularLocal residual gap (L1537)
74	
75	**Context**: After the case split on `x ∈ 𝔭` (where `𝔭 ⊆ (x)` is a minimal
76	prime), the `x ∈ 𝔭` branch reduces to the claim **`(x) ∈ minimalPrimes R`
77	contradicts the regular local structure**.
78	
79	**Why it's hard**:
80	
81	- The Stacks 00NQ proof uses `lemma-regular-graded`:
82	  `gr_𝔪(R) ≅ κ[X₁,…,X_d]` is a domain. Then `x ∉ 𝔪²` implies `x̄ ≠ 0` in
83	  `gr_𝔪(R)`, so `x` is "regular" in the graded sense, hence `(x)` cannot be
84	  a minimal prime (which would make `x` nilpotent, hence `x̄^n = 0` in the
85	  domain `gr_𝔪(R)`, hence `x̄ = 0`, contradiction).
86	- Mathlib at the pinned commit `b80f227` does **not** expose the graded
87	  ring `gr_𝔪(R)` or its identification with the polynomial ring for
88	  regular local rings. Standalone formalization of this is ~500-800 LOC.
89	
90	**Alternative bypass attempts considered (all blocked)**:
91	
92	1. **Pre-pick `x ∉` all minimal primes via prime avoidance**: requires
93	   `Ideal.subset_union_prime_finite` + `minimalPrimes` finite, doable in
94	   Mathlib. But this changes the `𝔭` ⊆ `(x)` relationship: with this `x`,
95	   minimal primes of R need not be `⊆ (x)`. The iteration argument
96	   `𝔭 ⊆ x·𝔭` requires `𝔭 ⊆ (x)`, so prime avoidance doesn't directly
97	   eliminate the case.
98	
99	2. **Dimension formula** `dim R/𝔭 + height 𝔭 ≤ dim R`: gives `dim R/(x) ≤
100	   dim R = k + 1` when `(x)` is minimal. Combined with `dim R/(x) = k`
101	   (from helper 2), the inequality `k ≤ k+1` is consistent, no
102	   contradiction.
103	
104	3. **Direct nilpotence of `x`**: if `(x) ∈ minimalPrimes R`, then
105	   `x ∈ nilradical R`, so `x^n = 0` for some `n ≥ 2` (since `x ≠ 0`). This
106	   makes `x` a zero-divisor but does not directly contradict `x ∉ 𝔪²`.
107

4	
5	### Attempt 1
6	- **Approach:** Build an explicit `kbar`-AlgHom `φ : (Away f) ⊗[kbar] (GmRing kbar) →ₐ[kbar] L`
7	  where `L := Localization.Away (X () : MvPolynomial Unit (Away f))`, then construct
8	  a left-inverse `RingHom` `ψ_ring : L →+* (Away f) ⊗[kbar] (GmRing kbar)` and conclude
9	  injectivity of `φ` via `Function.Injective.isDomain`.
10	  - `L` is a domain (localization of polynomial ring over a domain at non-zero-divisor).
11	  - `φ` lifts the pair `(f1_kbar, f2)` via `Algebra.TensorProduct.lift`:
12	    - `f1_kbar : (Away f) →ₐ[kbar] L` via `Algebra.ofId`,
13	    - `f2 : GmRing kbar →ₐ[kbar] L` via `IsLocalization.Away.lift` after showing
14	      that `X () : MvPolynomial Unit kbar` maps to a unit in `L` (it does — the image
15	      is the localized element via `polkToLocAwayxS`).
16	  - `ψ_ring : L →+* target` via `IsLocalization.Away.lift`, where the base map is
17	    `s_to_target = Algebra.TensorProduct.map (AlgHom.id A) (algebraMap Polk Gm)`.
18	    The image of `xS = 1 ⊗ₜ X ()` is a unit (its inverse is `1 ⊗ₜ (X ())⁻¹` in Gm).
19	  - To verify `ψ_ring ∘ φ = id` on `(Away f) ⊗[kbar] (GmRing kbar)`:
20	    - On `a ⊗ b`: `φ (a ⊗ b) = f1_kbar a * f2 b`, then `ψ_ring (f1_kbar a) = a ⊗ 1`
21	      (Helper B) and `ψ_ring (f2 b) = 1 ⊗ b` (Helper D), so the product becomes
22	      `a ⊗ b` via `tmul_mul_tmul`.
23	    - Helper D follows from showing `ψ_ring ∘ f2 = includeRight` on the image of
24	      `algebraMap (MvPolynomial Unit kbar) (GmRing kbar)`, then applying
25	      `IsLocalization.ringHom_ext` for the localization `GmRing kbar`.

18	
19	### Attempt 1
20	- **Approach:** Reuse the iter-184 closed proof body directly. The original closure was
21	  ```
22	  rw [← Scheme.Hom.coe_opensRange (Proj.awayι _ _ _ _), Proj.opensRange_awayι]
23	  rintro _ ⟨x, rfl⟩
24	  rw [SetLike.mem_coe, ← Scheme.Hom.mem_preimage]
25	  change x ∈ Proj.fromOfGlobalSections _ _ _ ⁻¹ᵁ Proj.basicOpen ...
26	  ...
27	  ```
28	- **Result:** FAILED — `rw [← Scheme.Hom.coe_opensRange (Proj.awayι _ _ _ _)]` did not unify
29	  the `Set.range ⇑(Proj.awayι ...)` pattern (typed at `Set ↥(Proj 𝒜)`) against the goal RHS
30	  (typed at `Set ↥(ProjectiveLineBar kbar).left`). `rw` does not unfold the `.left`/`asOver`
31	  projection.
32	- **Dead end:** Direct iter-184 body transplant.
33	
34	### Attempt 2 — RESOLVED
35	- **Approach:** Reshape the membership step-by-step via `change` instead of trying to
36	  rewrite `Set.range`. The chain:
37	  1. `rintro _ ⟨x, rfl⟩` to destructure the range subset.
38	  2. `change (ProjectiveLineBar.onePt kbar).left.base x ∈ (Proj.awayι _ _ _ _).opensRange.1`
39	     — defeq reshape from `Set.range` to `.opensRange.1`.
40	  3. `rw [Proj.opensRange_awayι]` — now rewrites the `.opensRange` cleanly to
41	     `Proj.basicOpen 𝒜 (X 1)`.
42	  4. `change x ∈ (ProjectiveLineBar.onePt kbar).left ⁻¹ᵁ Proj.basicOpen _ _` — flip
43	     to a preimage membership statement.
44	  5. `change x ∈ Proj.fromOfGlobalSections _ _ _ ⁻¹ᵁ _` — unfold `onePt.left` to its
45	     `Proj.fromOfGlobalSections` shape (the `pointOfVec` projection is defeq through
46	     `Over.homMk`).
47	  6. `rw [Proj.fromOfGlobalSections_preimage_basicOpen ...]` — reduces to a
48	     `(Spec k̄).basicOpen` membership on the evaluation `evalIntoGlobal v (X 1)`.
49	  7. `refine (Scheme.basicOpen_of_isUnit _ ?_).symm ▸ Opens.mem_top x` — basic open of
50	     a unit is `⊤`.
51	  8. Final unit check: `IsUnit ((ΓSpecIso _).inv.hom (eval (1,1) (X 1)))` reduces to
52	     `IsUnit 1` by `simp` (via `eval_X` + `map_one`).
53	- **Result:** RESOLVED. `#axioms iotaGm_r_1_range_subset = {propext, Classical.choice, Quot.sound}`.
54	- **Key insight:** The carrier-type mismatch between `↥(ProjectiveLineBar kbar).left`
55	  and `↥(Proj (projectiveLineBarGrading kbar))` is bridgeable by `change` (defeq through
56	  `Scheme.asOver` / `OverClass.asOver`) but NOT by `rw`. The fix is structural: rephrase
57	  the membership goal via `(...).opensRange.1` reshape BEFORE invoking
58	  `Proj.opensRange_awayι`. This makes the propagated `iotaGm_r_1` def and `iotaGm_r_1_fac`
59	  lemma axiom-clean as well.

2	
3	## Mode: cross-domain-inspiration
4	
5	The Lane A.3.i route (`Picard/IdentityComponent.lean`) is CHURNING per
6	the iter-190 progress-critic: net +3 sorries from phase entry, 16
7	helpers added across 4 iters, HARD SCOPE CAP escalation triggered with
8	0 closures in iter-189. The recurring blocker is the absence of an
9	EGA IV₂ 4.5.8 analog (scheme-level connectedness of products) at
10	Mathlib b80f227. This blocks closure of `isSubgroupHomomorphism`,
11	which in turn blocks `isFiniteTypeGeometricallyIrreducible` and
12	`baseChangeIso`. Continuing to add scaffolds without addressing the
13	substrate is the failure pattern.
14	
15	## Structural problem
16	
17	The project needs, for a connected group object `G` in `Sch/k` with
18	geometrically connected identity component `G^0 ⊂ G`:
19	
20	1. **`G^0 × G^0` is connected** (so its image under
21	   `G × G → G, (a,b) ↦ a · b^{-1}` lands in `G^0`, the connected
22	   component containing the identity).
23	2. **A `GrpObj`-structure on `G^0`** inherited from `GrpObj G`
24	   restricted to the connected component.
25	
26	The first ingredient (scheme-level product-of-connected-is-connected
27	under suitable geometric hypotheses) is what EGA IV₂ Prop 4.5.8 provides.
28	The second ingredient is the `Scheme.GrpObjAsOverPullback` / `OverClass`
29	bridging issue: Mathlib has
30	`Scheme.GrpObjAsOverPullback : [GrpObj (asOver M S)] → GrpObj (asOver
31	(pullback (M ↘ S) f) T)` but the project's `Over.mk (pullback.snd
32	G.hom φ)` shape doesn't directly unify with the `asOver M S` shape
33	without explicit `OverClass` bridging.
34	
35	## Failed approaches
36

3	\chapter{Vanishing of \(H^1\) for skyscraper sheaves on a curve (RR.2.H\(^1\))}
4	\label{chap:RR_H1Vanishing}
5	
6	% SOURCE: Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, II.1, Exercise~1.16
7	% (flasque sheaves), Exercise~1.17 (skyscraper sheaves); III.2,
8	% Proposition~2.5 (flasque sheaves are acyclic) (read from
9	% references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 67--68 and
10	% 225). All verbatim quotes pasted in the declaration blocks below were
11	% copied character-by-character from the rendered page images of the
12	% scanned PDF (no text layer is present).
13	
14	\section{Setup and motivation}
15	
16	This chapter is the project-side build of the
17	\texttt{RR.2.H\(^1\)} sub-phase, committed in iter-188 of the strategy
18	revision: \emph{the first cohomology of a closed-point skyscraper sheaf
19	on a smooth proper curve vanishes}. Concretely, the chapter pins the
20	identity
21	\[
22	  \dim_{\bar k}\, H^1\bigl(C,\, \mathtt{skyscraperSheaf}\;P\;
23	    (\Module.\mathtt{of}\;\bar k\;\bar k)\bigr) \;=\; 0
24	\]
25	on a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\), where
26	\(P \in C\) is a closed point and the cohomology is computed through the
27	project's \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured \(\Ext\)-cohomology pipeline
28	\(\HModule \bar k\) (chapter~REF, sibling
29	\texttt{Cohomology\_StructureSheafModuleK.tex}).
30	
31	\paragraph{Why a dedicated chapter.} Mathlib (snapshot
32	\texttt{b80f227}) does \emph{not} ship a flasque-cohomology vanishing
33	lemma at the \(\Ext\)-cohomology level used by
34	\(\HModule \bar k\), nor a Serre-duality theorem for curves. The
35	genus-\(0\) critical path nevertheless needs the skyscraper
36	\(H^1\)-vanishing twice: once inside the Hartshorne~IV.1.3
37	\(\chi\)-additivity assembly
38	(\cref{chap:RiemannRoch_RRFormula}, the parent lemma
39	\texttt{eulerCharacteristic\_skyscraperSheaf}), and once inside the
40	\texttt{RR.3} chain that produces a non-constant section
41	(\cref{chap:RiemannRoch_OCofP}, the downstream consumer
42	\texttt{h1\_vanishing\_genusZero}). The present chapter unifies the
43	substrate behind both consumers into a single short, self-contained
44	project-side build.
45	
46	\paragraph{Strategy of the chapter.} The argument is the classical
47	Hartshorne~III~\S2 one: an injective resolution of a flasque sheaf
48	yields a flasque quotient at each stage (since both the injective object
49	and the original sheaf are flasque), and the global-sections functor is
50	exact on a short exact sequence of flasque sheaves. Iterating, every
51	derived functor of \(\Gamma\) vanishes on a flasque input in positive
52	degree. The chapter then specialises to the closed-point skyscraper
53	sheaf, which is flasque by inspection (it is the pushforward, along the
54	inclusion of a one-point closed subspace, of the constant sheaf on an
55	irreducible base — and pushforwards of flasque sheaves are flasque).
56	
57	\paragraph{Standing hypotheses.} Throughout this chapter, \(\bar k\) is
58	an algebraically closed field and \(C\) is a smooth proper geometrically
59	irreducible curve over \(\bar k\). The Lean signatures (planned location
60	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/H1Vanishing.lean}) carry the same
61	typeclass package as the rest of the project's curve API
62	(\(\mathtt{IsProper}\), \(\mathtt{SmoothOfRelativeDimension}\, 1\),
63	\(\mathtt{GeometricallyIrreducible}\), \(\mathtt{IsIntegral}\)). The
64	flasque-vanishing lemma of \S2 below is stated more generally for a
65	noetherian topological space (the natural generality of
66	Hartshorne~III.2.5); the curve specialisation is read off by instance
67	synthesis on the underlying space \(C.\mathtt{left.toTopCat}\).
68	
69	\section{Flasque sheaves and their cohomology vanishing}
70	\label{sec:H1Vanishing_flasque}
71	
72	\begin{definition}
73	[Flasque sheaf]
74	  \label{def:isFlasque_sheaf}
75	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IsFlasque}

89	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.1, Exercise~1.16 (definition); recalled

99	  \(\mathcal F : \mathrm{Sheaf}\;J\;(\Module \bar k)\), where \(J\) is the
100	  Grothendieck topology of opens on the underlying topological space of

# Strategy Critic — iter-191

You are the strategy critic. Read the current STRATEGY.md (verbatim
below), the project's goal statement, the references summary, and a
blueprint summary (chapter titles + one-line topic per chapter).
Challenge the strategy as a fresh mathematician would. NO sunk-cost
loyalty.

## Project goal (verbatim, references/challenge.lean header)

Formalize Christian Merten's Jacobian challenge: nine protected
declarations headlined by `AlgebraicGeometry.Jacobian` and
`Jacobian.nonempty_jacobianWitness` — existence of an Albanese /
Jacobian object uniform over the `k`-rational pointing of a smooth
proper geometrically irreducible curve `C / k`, with **no**
`C(k) ≠ ∅` hypothesis. End-state contract: zero inline `sorry`,
kernel-only axioms (`propext`, `Classical.choice`, `Quot.sound`).
Char-general: the protected signatures take `[Field k]` only — NO
`CharZero`.

## STRATEGY.md (verbatim)

```markdown
$(cat /home/archon/FormalizationProjects/Algebraic-Jacobian-Challenge/.archon/STRATEGY.md)
```

## References summary

```markdown
$(cat /home/archon/FormalizationProjects/Algebraic-Jacobian-Challenge/references/summary.md)
```

## Blueprint summary

Chapter titles + one-line topic per chapter (from
`blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`):

See `blueprint/src/chapters/` directory listing for the canonical
roster (one chapter per Lean file; consolidated chapters declared via
`% archon:covers ... .lean`).

## Specific re-validation requests (live challenges from prior iters)

1. **Lane M↓ Option (a) viability**: iter-190 strategy-critic REJECTED
   permanent-named-sorry Option (c) as goal-incompatible
   (kernel-only-axioms clause excludes `sorryAx`). The plan agent
   re-opened Lane M↓ as project-side Option (a) build (~8-15 iters /
   ~150-300 LOC) targeting Stacks 00TT `isRegularLocalRing_stalk_of_smooth`
   via the smooth → flat → polynomial presentation → regular sequence
   chain. Is this viable, or does Mathlib b80f227 lack a load-bearing
   substrate that makes Option (a) also impractical? If impractical,
   what's the next-best route?

2. **A.3.0 substrate (scheme-level tangent space ↔ first-order
   deformations)**: enumerated as new substrate row iter-190.
   Estimated ~6-10 iters / ~200-400 LOC. The substrate is shared by
   A.3.iii (tangent iso H¹(O_C) ≅ T_0 Pic⁰) and A.3.iv (Pic⁰
   smoothness). Is this enumeration accurate, or does the substrate
   need further decomposition before bodies can start?

3. **A.4.d divisor-map UP route**: pivoted iter-188 from Sym^g to
   divisor-map. Chapter rewrite is still deferred (iter-191+ writer
   target). Is the pivot strategically sound, or does the deferred
   chapter mask hidden gaps?

4. **Lane A.3.i CHURNING (4 iters, 0 closures iter-189-190)**:
   mathlib-analogist `lane-a3i-isconnected-prod` dispatched iter-191
   plan-phase to find EGA IV₂ 4.5.8 cross-domain analogues. If the
   analogist returns "no Mathlib analog, build the substrate
   project-side", is the route still viable in <20 iters total, or
   does the strategy need a fundamental re-think?

5. **Genus-0 RR.2.H¹ project-side build**: committed iter-188; chapter
   `RR_H1Vanishing` landed iter-190 plan-phase (560 lines). H¹ vanishing
   for closed-point skyscraper sheaves on a smooth proper curve via
   flasque resolutions (Hartshorne III.2.5 specialised). Estimated
   ~8-12 iters / ~200-400 LOC. Is this viable at the Project's
   `Module k̄`-flavoured `H^Module k̄`-Ext cohomology pipeline, or does
   the cohomology framework introduce a hidden gap?

## Expected output

Per the strategy-critic descriptor: a fresh-context verdict, either
**SOUND** (with any minor observations) or **CHALLENGE** (with named
revisions: route swaps, phase decomposition, substrate enumeration,
estimation revisions). The plan agent MUST either update STRATEGY.md
to address each CHALLENGE or write an explicit rebuttal in
`iter/iter-191/plan.md`.

Format compliance: STRATEGY.md is currently 12.2 KB / 145 lines per
iter-190. The canonical structure (skeleton in `prompts/plan.md`
"Long-arc Strategy") permits ~12 KB / ~250 lines max. Any new
substrate row added to fix a CHALLENGE must displace another row OR
fit within the existing budget; the file shrinks toward "complete",
does NOT grow.

NO iter-by-iter narrative in your output — that's iter sidecar content.
Stay focused on strategic shape.

100	  Grothendieck topology of opens on the underlying topological space of
101	  the curve \(C\). The surjectivity condition is read on the underlying
102	  presheaf of \(\bar k\)-modules; the project predicate
103	  \texttt{Scheme.IsFlasque} packages exactly this condition.
104	
105	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean predicate
106	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IsFlasque} takes a sheaf
107	  \(\mathcal F : \mathrm{Sheaf}\;(\Opens.\mathtt{grothendieckTopology}\;X)\;
108	  (\Module \bar k)\) and asserts that for every pair \(V \le U\) of opens
109	  of \(X\), the restriction morphism
110	  \((\mathcal F.\mathtt{val.map}\;(\mathrm{leOp}\;V\;U)) :
111	  \mathcal F.\mathtt{val.obj}\;(\mathrm{op}\;U) \to
112	  \mathcal F.\mathtt{val.obj}\;(\mathrm{op}\;V)\) is surjective as a map of
113	  \(\bar k\)-modules.
114	\end{definition}
115	
116	\begin{lemma}
117	[Pushforward of a flasque sheaf is flasque]
118	  \label{lem:isFlasque_pushforward}
119	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IsFlasque.pushforward}
120	  \uses{def:isFlasque_sheaf}
121	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.1, Exercise~1.16(d) (read from
122	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 67).
123	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "If \(f: X \to Y\) is a continuous map, and if
124	  % \(\mathscr{F}\) is a flasque sheaf on \(X\), then \(f_*\mathscr{F}\)
125	  % is a flasque sheaf on \(Y\)."
126	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.1, Exercise~1.16(d).}
127	  Let \(f \colon X \to Y\) be a continuous map of topological spaces and
128	  let \(\mathcal F\) be a flasque sheaf on \(X\). Then the pushforward
129	  sheaf \(f_*\mathcal F\) is flasque on \(Y\).
130	\end{lemma}
131	
132	\begin{proof}
133	  \uses{def:isFlasque_sheaf}
134	  For an inclusion of opens \(V \subseteq U\) in \(Y\), the restriction
135	  map of \(f_*\mathcal F\) is by definition the restriction map of
136	  \(\mathcal F\) on the open sets \(f^{-1}(V) \subseteq f^{-1}(U)\) of
137	  \(X\):
138	  \[
139	    (f_*\mathcal F)(U) \;=\; \mathcal F(f^{-1}(U))
140	    \;\xrightarrow{\;\mathcal F(f^{-1}(V) \subseteq f^{-1}(U))\;}\;
141	    \mathcal F(f^{-1}(V)) \;=\; (f_*\mathcal F)(V).
142	  \]
143	  Since \(\mathcal F\) is flasque on \(X\), the right-hand restriction is
144	  surjective, hence so is the left-hand one. Therefore \(f_*\mathcal F\)
145	  is flasque on \(Y\).
146	\end{proof}
147	
148	\begin{lemma}
149	[Constant sheaf on an irreducible space is flasque]
150	  \label{lem:isFlasque_constant_irreducible}
151	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IsFlasque.constant_of_irreducible}
152	  \uses{def:isFlasque_sheaf}
153	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.1, Exercise~1.16(a) (read from
154	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 67).
155	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Show that a constant sheaf on an irreducible
156	  % topological space is flasque. See (I, \S1) for irreducible
157	  % topological spaces."
158	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.1, Exercise~1.16(a).}
159	  Let \(X\) be an irreducible topological space and let \(A\) be a
160	  \(\bar k\)-module. Then the constant sheaf
161	  \((\mathrm{constantSheaf}\;J\;\_)\;A\) on \(X\) is flasque.
162	\end{lemma}
163	
164	\begin{proof}
165	  \uses{def:isFlasque_sheaf}
166	  On an irreducible topological space every non-empty open is connected
167	  and dense, so the constant presheaf
168	  \(U \mapsto A\) (with all non-empty restriction maps the identity)
169	  already satisfies the sheaf condition, and equals its sheafification.
170	  For every inclusion of non-empty opens \(V \subseteq U\), the
171	  restriction map is the identity on \(A\) — in particular surjective.
172	  For an inclusion involving the empty open, the target is the zero
173	  module, so surjectivity is immediate. Therefore the constant sheaf is
174	  flasque.
175	\end{proof}
176	
177	\begin{theorem}
178	[Flasque sheaves have vanishing higher cohomology]
179	  \label{thm:H1_vanishing_flasque}
180	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.HModule_flasque_eq_zero}
181	  \uses{def:isFlasque_sheaf}
182	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], III.2, Proposition~2.5 (read from
183	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 225).
184	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Proposition 2.5. \emph{If \(\mathscr{F}\) is a
185	  % flasque sheaf on a topological space \(X\), then
186	  % \(H^i(X, \mathscr{F}) = 0\) for all \(i > 0\).}"
187	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, III.2, Proposition~2.5, p.~208.}
188	  Let \(X\) be a topological space and let \(\mathcal F\) be a flasque
189	  sheaf of \(\bar k\)-modules on \(X\), viewed as an object of
190	  \(\mathrm{Sheaf}\;(\Opens.\mathtt{grothendieckTopology}\;X)\;
191	  (\Module \bar k)\). Then, for every \(i \ge 1\),
192	  \[
193	    \HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal F\;i \;=\; 0.
194	  \]
195	  In particular, \(\dim_{\bar k} \HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal F\;1 = 0\).
196	\end{theorem}
197	
198	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Proof. Embed \(\mathscr{F}\) in an injective
199	% object \(\mathscr{I}\) of \(\mathfrak{Ab}(X)\) and let \(\mathscr{G}\)
200	% be the quotient:
201	% \(0 \to \mathscr{F} \to \mathscr{I} \to \mathscr{G} \to 0\).
202	% Then \(\mathscr{F}\) is flasque by hypothesis, \(\mathscr{I}\) is
203	% flasque by (2.4), and so \(\mathscr{G}\) is flasque by (II, Ex. 1.16c).
204	% Now since \(\mathscr{F}\) is flasque, we have an exact sequence (II,
205	% Ex. 1.16b)
206	% \(0 \to \Gamma(X, \mathscr{F}) \to \Gamma(X, \mathscr{I}) \to
207	% \Gamma(X, \mathscr{G}) \to 0\).
208	% On the other hand, since \(\mathscr{I}\) is injective, we have
209	% \(H^i(X, \mathscr{I}) = 0\) for \(i > 0\) (1.1Ae). Thus from the long
210	% exact sequence of cohomology, we get \(H^1(X, \mathscr{F}) = 0\) and
211	% \(H^i(X, \mathscr{F}) \cong H^{i-1}(X, \mathscr{G})\) for each
212	% \(i \geq 2\). But \(\mathscr{G}\) is also flasque, so by induction on
213	% \(i\) we get the result."
214	\begin{proof}
215	  \uses{def:isFlasque_sheaf}
216	  We follow Hartshorne's induction. Embed \(\mathcal F\) into an
217	  injective object \(\mathcal I\) of the abelian category
218	  \(\mathrm{Sheaf}\;(\Opens.\mathtt{grothendieckTopology}\;X)\;
219	  (\Module \bar k)\) — such an injective exists by the
220	  enough-injectives instance for sheaves of \(\bar k\)-modules on opens
221	  (\cref{chap:Cohomology_StructureSheafModuleK}). Form the quotient
222	  short exact sequence
223	  \[
224	    0 \to \mathcal F \to \mathcal I \to \mathcal G \to 0
225	  \]
226	  in the sheaf category.
227	
228	  \emph{Step 1: the quotient \(\mathcal G\) is flasque.} The hypothesis
229	  gives that \(\mathcal F\) is flasque. The injective object
230	  \(\mathcal I\) is flasque by the Hartshorne~III~Lemma~2.4 input:
231	  any injective \(\mathcal O_X\)-module is flasque, where the
232	  \(\mathcal O_X\) here is the constant sheaf of rings \(\bar k\) on
233	  \(X\), so the sheaf category over the constant sheaf of rings is the
234	  category of sheaves of \(\bar k\)-modules and the lemma applies. From
235	  the two flasqueness inputs the quotient \(\mathcal G\) inherits
236	  flasqueness by Hartshorne~II~Exercise~1.16(c): \emph{if
237	  \(0 \to \mathcal F' \to \mathcal F \to \mathcal F'' \to 0\) is an
238	  exact sequence and \(\mathcal F', \mathcal F\) are flasque, then
239	  \(\mathcal F''\) is flasque}.
240	
241	  \emph{Step 2: the global-sections functor is exact on the chosen
242	  short exact sequence.} Since the leftmost term \(\mathcal F\) is
243	  flasque, the long exact sequence of cohomology starts with a short
244	  exact sequence on global sections
245	  (Hartshorne~II~Exercise~1.16(b)):
246	  \[
247	    0 \to \Gamma(X, \mathcal F) \to \Gamma(X, \mathcal I) \to
248	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal G) \to 0.
249	  \]
250	
251	  \emph{Step 3: the long exact sequence gives the vanishing.}
252	  The object \(\mathcal I\) is injective in the abelian sheaf category,
253	  hence its right-derived global-sections cohomology vanishes in
254	  positive degree:
255	  \(\HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal I\;i = 0\) for all \(i \ge 1\). The long
256	  exact sequence of the short exact sequence above, applied to the
257	  derived functor \(\HModule\;\bar k\;{-}\;{\bullet}\) of \(\Gamma\),
258	  reads in degrees \(0\) and \(1\) as
259	  \[
260	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal I) \to \Gamma(X, \mathcal G)
261	    \to \HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal F\;1
262	    \to \HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal I\;1 = 0.
263	  \]
264	  The leftmost map is surjective by Step~2, so the connecting morphism
265	  to \(\HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal F\;1\) is the zero map, and the kernel
266	  of \(\HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal F\;1 \to 0\) is all of
267	  \(\HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal F\;1\); hence
268	  \(\HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal F\;1 = 0\).
269	
270	  \emph{Step 4: higher degrees by induction.} For \(i \ge 2\), the long
271	  exact sequence yields
272	  \(\HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal F\;i \cong \HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal G\;
273	  (i - 1)\); the inductive hypothesis on the flasque quotient
274	  \(\mathcal G\) gives the right-hand side is zero. This closes the
275	  vanishing in all positive degrees. The chapter consumes only the
276	  degree-\(1\) instance, but the lemma is stated and proved in full
277	  generality so it can support later sub-builds (e.g.\ Grothendieck
278	  vanishing on a one-dimensional scheme).
279	
280	  % NOTE: the proof structure mirrors Hartshorne~III~Proposition~2.5
281	  % verbatim. The Lean closure is gated on three substrate inputs:
282	  % (a) the enough-injectives instance for `Sheaf J (ModuleCat kbar)`
283	  % already provided by chapter
284	  % `Cohomology_StructureSheafModuleK.tex`; (b) the project-side
285	  % flasque-quotient lemma (Hartshorne~II~Ex.~1.16(c)) restated for
286	  % `ModuleCat kbar`-valued sheaves; (c) the long-exact-sequence
287	  % carrier for `Abelian.Ext.covariantSequence` at the `HModule kbar`
288	  % level (the same LES carrier consumed by
289	  % `eulerCharacteristic_shortExact_add` in
290	  % `RiemannRoch_RRFormula.tex`). All three are project-side ~50-100
291	  % LOC apiece.
292	\end{proof}
293	
294	\section{Skyscraper sheaves are flasque}
295	\label{sec:H1Vanishing_skyscraperFlasque}
296	
297	\begin{lemma}
298	[Skyscraper sheaf as pushforward from a closed point]
299	  \label{lem:skyscraperSheaf_eq_pushforward}
300	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.skyscraperSheaf_eq_pushforward_const}
301	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.1, Exercise~1.17 (read from
302	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 68).
303	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Skyscraper Sheaves. Let \(X\) be a topological space,
304	  % let \(P\) be a point, and let \(A\) be an abelian group. Define a
305	  % sheaf \(i_P(A)\) on \(X\) as follows: \(i_P(A)(U) = A\) if \(P \in
306	  % U\), \(0\) otherwise. \ldots Show that this sheaf could also be
307	  % described as \(i_*(A)\), where \(A\) denotes the constant sheaf
308	  % \(A\) on the closed subspace \(\{P\}^-\), and \(i\colon \{P\}^- \to
309	  % X\) is the inclusion."
310	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.1, Exercise~1.17.}
311	  Let \(X\) be a topological space, let \(P \in X\) be a point with
312	  closure \(\overline{\{P\}}\), and let \(A\) be a \(\bar k\)-module.
313	  Write \(i \colon \overline{\{P\}} \hookrightarrow X\) for the closed
314	  embedding. Then the skyscraper sheaf
315	  \(\mathtt{skyscraperSheaf}\;P\;A\) on \(X\) is naturally isomorphic to
316	  the pushforward \(i_*\bigl((\mathrm{constantSheaf}\;{-}\;\_)\;A\bigr)\)
317	  of the constant sheaf with value \(A\) on \(\overline{\{P\}}\).
318	\end{lemma}
319	
320	\begin{proof}
321	  Both presheaves send an open \(U \subseteq X\) to \(A\) when
322	  \(P \in U\) (equivalently, when
323	  \(U \cap \overline{\{P\}}\) is non-empty, since the closed subset
324	  \(\overline{\{P\}}\) is irreducible and any open meeting it contains
325	  \(P\)), and to \(0\) when \(P \notin U\) (equivalently,
326	  \(U \cap \overline{\{P\}} = \varnothing\)). The restriction maps agree
327	  by inspection: in the \(P \in V \subseteq U\) case both are the
328	  identity on \(A\); in the \(P \notin V\) case both factor through
329	  \(0\). The natural identification on opens is a sheaf morphism, and
330	  on stalks it is the identity (the stalk at any \(Q \neq P\) outside
331	  \(\overline{\{P\}}\) is zero on both sides; the stalk at any point of
332	  \(\overline{\{P\}}\) is \(A\) on both sides). Hence the comparison is
333	  an isomorphism of sheaves.
334	\end{proof}
335	
336	\begin{lemma}
337	[Closure of a closed point is irreducible]
338	  \label{lem:closedPoint_closure_irreducible}
339	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PrimeDivisor.closure_isIrreducible}
340	
341	  Let \(C\) be a Noetherian scheme (in particular the smooth proper
342	  curve \(C / \bar k\) of this chapter), and let \(P \in C\) be a closed
343	  point. Then the closure \(\overline{\{P\}} = \{P\}\) is irreducible
344	  (trivially: a singleton is irreducible as a topological space).
345	
346	  \textit{Project-bespoke ancillary} (immediate set-theoretic fact;
347	  no external source pinned).
348	\end{lemma}
349	
350	\begin{proof}
351	  A singleton topological space is irreducible: its only non-empty open
352	  is the whole space, which trivially intersects every other non-empty
353	  open in a non-empty set (in fact in itself). For a closed point
354	  \(P \in C\) the closure \(\overline{\{P\}}\) is \(\{P\}\), which is
355	  homeomorphic to a singleton, hence irreducible.
356	\end{proof}
357	
358	\begin{lemma}
359	[Closed-point skyscraper sheaf is flasque]
360	  \label{lem:skyscraperSheaf_isFlasque}
361	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.skyscraperSheaf_isFlasque}
362	  \uses{def:isFlasque_sheaf, lem:skyscraperSheaf_eq_pushforward,
363	        lem:closedPoint_closure_irreducible,
364	        lem:isFlasque_pushforward, lem:isFlasque_constant_irreducible}
365	
366	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
367	  \(\bar k\) and let \(P \in C\) be a closed point. Then the
368	  closed-point skyscraper sheaf
369	  \[
370	    k(P) \;:=\; \mathtt{skyscraperSheaf}\;P\;
371	      (\Module.\mathtt{of}\;\bar k\;\bar k)
372	  \]
373	  is flasque as a sheaf of \(\bar k\)-modules on \(C\).
374	
375	  \textit{Project-bespoke packaging} (no separately named external
376	  source; the constituent inputs are quoted in their own lemma blocks
377	  above).
378	\end{lemma}
379	
380	\begin{proof}
381	  \uses{def:isFlasque_sheaf, lem:skyscraperSheaf_eq_pushforward,
382	        lem:closedPoint_closure_irreducible,
383	        lem:isFlasque_pushforward, lem:isFlasque_constant_irreducible}
384	  By
385	  \cref{lem:skyscraperSheaf_eq_pushforward}, the skyscraper sheaf is
386	  isomorphic to the pushforward
387	  \(i_*\bigl((\mathrm{constantSheaf}\;{-}\;\_)\;
388	  (\Module.\mathtt{of}\;\bar k\;\bar k)\bigr)\) along the inclusion
389	  \(i \colon \overline{\{P\}} \hookrightarrow C\). The base
390	  \(\overline{\{P\}} = \{P\}\) is irreducible
391	  (\cref{lem:closedPoint_closure_irreducible}), so the constant sheaf on
392	  it is flasque (\cref{lem:isFlasque_constant_irreducible}). Pushforward
393	  of a flasque sheaf is flasque
394	  (\cref{lem:isFlasque_pushforward}). Flasqueness transports along the
395	  isomorphism of \cref{lem:skyscraperSheaf_eq_pushforward}, so
396	  \(k(P)\) is flasque.
397	\end{proof}
398	
399	\section{\(H^1\) of a closed-point skyscraper sheaf vanishes}
400	\label{sec:H1Vanishing_skyscraperH1}
401	
402	\begin{theorem}
403	
404	\leanok
405	[\(H^1\) of a closed-point skyscraper sheaf has dimension zero]
406	  \label{lem:H1_skyscraperSheaf_finrank_eq_zero_main}
407	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.H1_skyscraperSheaf_finrank_eq_zero}
408	  \uses{thm:H1_vanishing_flasque, lem:skyscraperSheaf_isFlasque}
409	
410	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
411	  \(\bar k\) and let \(P \in C\) be a closed point. Then
412	  \[
413	    \dim_{\bar k}\, H^1\bigl(C,\, \mathtt{skyscraperSheaf}\;P\;
414	      (\Module.\mathtt{of}\;\bar k\;\bar k)\bigr) \;=\; 0,
415	  \]
416	  where the left-hand side is the \(\bar k\)-finrank of
417	  \(\HModule\;\bar k\;(\mathtt{skyscraperSheaf}\;P\;(\Module.\mathtt{of}\;
418	  \bar k\;\bar k))\;1\).
419

484	On a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve `C / kbar`, the
485	Euler characteristic of the closed-point skyscraper sheaf
486	`skyscraperSheaf P.point (ModuleCat.of kbar kbar)` is `1`:
487	`χ(k(P)) = dim H⁰(C, k(P)) − dim H¹(C, k(P)) = 1 − 0 = 1`.

494	private theorem Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_skyscraperSheaf
495	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
496	    (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]
497	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]
498	    [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]
499	    (P : C.left.PrimeDivisor)
500	    [∀ (U : TopologicalSpace.Opens C.left), Decidable (P.point ∈ U)] :
501	    Scheme.eulerCharacteristic C
502	        (skyscraperSheaf (C := ModuleCat.{u} kbar) P.point
503	          (ModuleCat.of kbar kbar)) = 1 := by
504	  -- Unfold χ = dim H⁰ − dim H¹ and substitute the two halves.
505	  unfold Scheme.eulerCharacteristic
506	  -- Renormalise the goal with explicit `(C := ModuleCat.{u} kbar)` so the
507	  -- helper-conclusion `Module.finrank` matches under the `↑(·)` casts.
508	  change ((Module.finrank kbar
509	        (Scheme.HModule kbar
510	          (skyscraperSheaf (C := ModuleCat.{u} kbar) P.point
511	            (ModuleCat.of kbar kbar)) 0) : ℕ) : ℤ)
512	      - ((Module.finrank kbar
513	        (Scheme.HModule kbar
514	          (skyscraperSheaf (C := ModuleCat.{u} kbar) P.point
515	            (ModuleCat.of kbar kbar)) 1) : ℕ) : ℤ) = 1
516	  rw [Scheme.H0_skyscraperSheaf_finrank_eq_one C P,
517	    Scheme.H1_skyscraperSheaf_finrank_eq_zero C P]

2	\chapter{Weil divisors on a smooth proper curve (RR.1)}
3	\label{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}
4	% SOURCE: Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, II \S6, pp.~130--137 +
5	% IV.1, pp.~294--296 (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf).
6	% Stacks pointers (tag-level, not verbatim-quoted): 02RW (divisors),
7	% 02ME (order at a point on a regular 1-dimensional scheme),
8	% 0BE0 (degree of a divisor on a curve), 0BE3 (principal divisors have
9	% degree zero on a complete nonsingular curve).
10	\section{Setup and motivation}
11	This chapter is the first of four sub-build chapters \texttt{RR.1}--\texttt{RR.4}
12	that close the project's headline \emph{RR bridge}
13	\[
14	  \mathtt{genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}
15	  \,\colon\,
16	  \text{a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\) with
17	  \(g(C) = 0\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\).}
18	\]
19	The bridge as proved in Hartshorne (Example~IV.1.3.5) chains together:
20	divisor of a closed point on a curve (\texttt{RR.1}, this chapter); the
21	Riemann--Roch dimension formula \(\ell(D) = \deg D + 1\) for \(\deg D \geq 0\) on
22	a genus-\(0\) curve (\texttt{RR.2}); the global-sections argument identifying
23	\(\dim_k H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\) via \(1\) and a non-constant function
24	(\texttt{RR.3}); the ``rational curve \(\Rightarrow \cong \mathbb P^1\)''
25	classification recovered as a degree-\(1\) morphism into \(\mathbb P^1\) via
26	\texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} (\texttt{RR.4}).
27	Mathlib ships no \texttt{WeilDivisor} on a scheme. Adjacent pieces exist, but
28	none of them is the data structure we need: \texttt{MeromorphicOn.divisor}
29	(\texttt{Mathlib.Analysis.Meromorphic.Divisor}) is the order function of a
30	meromorphic function valued in a \emph{normed} field, not a formal sum of

660	% positive part of `principal (φ^* t_∞)` so that
661	% `lem:degree_via_pole_divisor` is mathematically correct (the principal
662	% divisor has degree 0 by `thm:principal_deg_zero`; only its
663	% positive part can have positive degree).
664	\begin{definition}
665	
666	[Positive part of a Weil divisor]
667	  \label{def:WeilDivisor_positivePart}
668	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.positivePart}
669	  \textit{Source: Project-bespoke (Hartshorne II.6.10 phrasing).}
670	  Let \(X\) be a scheme satisfying the codim-1 hypotheses of
671	  \ref{def:codim1_cycles}. For a Weil divisor
672	  \(D = \sum_Y n_Y \cdot Y \in \mathrm{Div}(X)\), the \emph{positive part}
673	  \((D)_0 \in \mathrm{Div}(X)\) is the Weil divisor
674	  \[
675	    (D)_0 \;:=\; \sum_{Y \,:\, n_Y > 0} n_Y \cdot Y.
676	  \]
677	  Concretely, \((D)_0\) is obtained from \(D\) by replacing each coefficient
678	  \(n_Y\) with \(\max(n_Y, 0)\). Equivalently, in the \(\Finsupp{\mathrm{PrimeDivisor}(X)}{\mathbb Z}\)
679	  presentation, the positive part is the join with the zero divisor in the
680	  lattice structure inherited from the pointwise order on \(\mathbb Z\):
681	  \(\,(D)_0 = D \sqcup 0\,\). The complementary \emph{negative part}
682	  \((D)_\infty\) is defined by \((D)_\infty := (-D)_0\), so that
683	  \(D = (D)_0 - (D)_\infty\) is the canonical decomposition into an
684	  effective (non-negative-coefficient) divisor minus an effective divisor.
685	  On a smooth proper curve \(C\) over \(\bar k\), where the prime divisors
686	  are the closed points and the coefficients are integers, the positive part
687	  reduces to keeping only the closed points whose multiplicity is positive
688	  — i.e. precisely the zeros of any rational function whose principal
689	  divisor is \(D\), in the case \(D = \mathrm{div}(f)\).
690	\end{definition}
691	\begin{lemma}
692	
693	\leanok
694	[Degree of the positive part of \(\mathrm{div}(f)\) for a non-constant rational
695	function equals the function-field extension degree]
696	  \label{lem:degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank}
697	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank}
698	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, Proposition~6.9, p.~137 (multiplicativity
699	  % of degree under finite pullback) (read from
700	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 154).
701	  % Stacks pointer: tag 02RW (pullback of divisors under finite
702	  % morphisms), tag 0BE6 (degree under finite morphism).
703	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Proposition 6.9. \emph{Let \(f \colon X \to Y\) be a finite
704	  % morphism of nonsingular curves. Then for any divisor \(D\) on \(Y\),
705	  % \(\deg(f^\ast D) = [K(X) : K(Y)] \cdot \deg D\).}"
706	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, Proposition~6.9, p.~137.}
707	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over an
708	  algebraically closed field \(\bar k\), and let
709	  \(\varphi \colon C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) be a non-constant morphism.
710	  Let \(t_\infty \in K(\mathbb P^1)\) be a local parameter at \(\infty \in \mathbb P^1\)
711	  (equivalently \(t_\infty = 1/u\) for the standard affine coordinate \(u\) on
712	  \(\mathbb P^1 \setminus \{\infty\}\)). Write \(f := \varphi^{\#} t_\infty
713	  \in K(C)\) for the pullback of \(t_\infty\) along \(\varphi\). Then \(f\) is
714	  nonzero (because \(\varphi\) is non-constant; equivalently \(\varphi^{-1}(\infty)\)
715	  is a proper closed subscheme of \(C\)) and the degree of the positive
716	  part of the principal divisor of \(f\) equals the function-field
717	  extension degree:
718	  \[
719	    \deg\bigl((\mathrm{div}(f))_0\bigr)
720	    \;=\; \bigl[\,K(C) \,:\, \bar k(\mathbb P^1)\,\bigr]
721	    \;=\; \mathrm{Module.finrank}_{\bar k(\mathbb P^1)} K(C).
722	  \]
723	  Equivalently, \((\mathrm{div}(f))_0 = \varphi^{*}[\infty]\) (the effective
724	  preimage divisor of \(\infty\)) and \(\deg(\varphi^{*}[\infty]) = \deg(\varphi)\).
725	\end{lemma}
726	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "PROOF. The morphism \(f \colon X \to Y\) is finite,
727	% so locally on \(Y\) corresponds to a finite extension of Dedekind domains
728	% \(A \subset B\). For each closed point \(P \in Y\) the ramification--inertia
729	% formula gives \(\sum_{Q | P} e_Q f_Q = [K(X) : K(Y)]\), where the sum runs
730	% over closed points \(Q \in X\) above \(P\). Summing this identity over
731	% \(P \in Y\) using the affine cover and noting that \(D = \sum_P n_P [P]\)
732	% gives \(\deg(f^\ast D) = \sum_{P} n_P \sum_{Q | P} e_Q f_Q
733	% = \sum_P n_P [K(X) : K(Y)] = [K(X) : K(Y)] \deg(D)\)."
734	\begin{proof}
735	  Following Hartshorne's Proposition~II.6.9 specialised to the divisor
736	  \(D = [\infty]\) on \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\). A non-constant morphism
737	  between smooth proper curves over \(\bar k\) is automatically finite (the
738	  image is a closed irreducible subset that is not a point and not empty,
739	  hence all of \(\mathbb P^1\); a surjective morphism between smooth proper
740	  irreducible curves of equal dimension is proper + quasi-finite, hence
741	  finite; cf.\ Hartshorne II.6.8). Hence the function-field extension
742	  \(\bar k(\mathbb P^1) \hookrightarrow K(C)\) is finite of degree
743	  \(d := [K(C) : \bar k(\mathbb P^1)] = \dim_{\bar k(\mathbb P^1)} K(C)\).
744	  Pick an affine open \(\mathrm{Spec}(A) \subset \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) containing
745	  \(\infty\); explicitly take \(A = \bar k[t_\infty]\) the standard chart at
746	  \(\infty\), with \(\infty\) corresponding to the maximal ideal
747	  \(\mathfrak m_\infty = (t_\infty) \subset A\). The preimage
748	  \(\mathrm{Spec}(B) := \varphi^{-1}(\mathrm{Spec}(A))\) is an affine open of \(C\)
749	  with \(B\) a finite (and integrally closed, by \(C\) smooth) extension of

# Refactor Directive

## Slug

lane-i-positivepart-clash-fix

## Problem

The iter-190 paired Lane I prover dispatch produced an integration
build RED state: `AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RationalCurveIso.lean`
and `AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/WeilDivisor.lean` BOTH define the
same fully-qualified declaration
`AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.positivePart`, with the
RationalCurveIso version `private`. Lean's namespace resolution
treats `private` as a visibility modifier only — fully-qualified names
must still be unique, so the redeclaration is rejected at link time:

```
error: AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RationalCurveIso.lean:416:26:
  a non-private declaration `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.positivePart`
  has already been declared
```

`lake build AlgebraicJacobian` exits 1 on this; the 10-consecutive-
zero-axiom build streak is interrupted until this clash resolves.

Additionally, the public pin
`AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank`
in WeilDivisor.lean landed in EXISTENTIAL form
(`∃ t halg, deg(positivePart(principal (algebraMap K K(C) t) halg)) = finrank`)
for soundness — the equation form is false-as-stated for arbitrary
`t`. But this existential form does not directly support
`Hom.poleDivisor_degree_eq_finrank`'s consumer (which needs the
equation at the SPECIFIC local-parameter witness `localParameterAtInfty
kbar`). The cleanest resolution is to refactor the public pin
signature to take the local-parameter hypothesis as an EXPLICIT
argument (equation form, no existential), and update the consumer in
RationalCurveIso.lean to feed `localParameterAtInfty kbar` plus its
local-parameter proof.

## Mathematical Justification

The mathematical content of Hartshorne II.6.9 is the equation
`deg((div(φ^* t))_0) = [K(C) : K(ℙ¹)]` at any uniformiser `t` of `O_{ℙ¹,
∞}`. The existential form picks SOME local parameter; the equation
form takes the local parameter as a hypothesis. Both are
mathematically equivalent (the existential is the projection of the
equation form onto the existence-of-witness fragment), but the
equation form is what the consumer site `Hom.poleDivisor` needs
(`Hom.poleDivisor φ := positivePart (principal (algebraMap _ _
(localParameterAtInfty kbar).val) halg)` evaluates at a SPECIFIC
witness, not at a chosen one).

Per iter-190 review recommendations §4: option (i) re-tightening the
Lean signature is preferred over option (ii) re-writing the chapter
prose, because the chapter prose is mathematically natural in
equation form (Hartshorne II.6.9 verbatim) and the soundness concern
is addressed by adding the explicit "is a local parameter" hypothesis.

The file-local typed-sorry pin
`WeilDivisor.degree_positivePart_principal_localParameterAtInfty_eq_finrank`
(L444 of RationalCurveIso.lean, currently `private`) IS the equation
form specialised to `t = (localParameterAtInfty kbar).val`. After
this refactor, the consumer in `Hom.poleDivisor_degree_eq_finrank`
will:
1. Use the public `Scheme.WeilDivisor.positivePart` (no file-local
   shadow).
2. Apply the public `Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank`
   reshaped to take `(localParameterAtInfty kbar).val` as the local-
   parameter argument.

The file-local pin can be removed entirely; the public pin in its
reshaped form is the single source of truth.

## Changes Requested

### File: `AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/WeilDivisor.lean`

Reshape `Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank`
(L543) from existential form to equation form with an explicit
local-parameter hypothesis.

- **Old** (L543–L555, existential form):
  ```
  theorem degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank
      {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
      (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]
      [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom] [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom]
      [IsIntegral C.left] [IsLocallyNoetherian C.left]
      [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne C.left]
      {K : Type u} [Field K] [Algebra K C.left.functionField]
      [Module.Finite K C.left.functionField] :
      ∃ (t : K) (halg : algebraMap K C.left.functionField t ≠ 0),
        degree (positivePart
          (principal (algebraMap K C.left.functionField t) halg)) =
          (Module.finrank K C.left.functionField : ℤ) := by
    sorry
  ```

- **New** (equation form, explicit local-parameter `t` argument):
  ```
  theorem degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank
      {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
      (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]
      [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom] [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom]
      [IsIntegral C.left] [IsLocallyNoetherian C.left]
      [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne C.left]
      {K : Type u} [Field K] [Algebra K C.left.functionField]
      [Module.Finite K C.left.functionField]
      (t : K)
      (halg : algebraMap K C.left.functionField t ≠ 0) :
      degree (positivePart
        (principal (algebraMap K C.left.functionField t) halg)) =
        (Module.finrank K C.left.functionField : ℤ) := by
    sorry
  ```

  Justification: The equation form takes `t` and `halg` as explicit
  parameters (matching the file-local file-local pin signature
  shape). The signature is now usable directly by any consumer with a
  concrete witness `t`; existence-of-witness is no longer the API
  surface (it's downstream of the consumer's choice).

  NOTE: this changes the type semantically — but since the body is a
  `sorry` (typed-sorry pin; body owed iter-191+ via Hartshorne II.6.9
  affine-chart `Ideal.sum_ramification_inertia` chain), the body
  signature is the only API surface and it is safe to reshape now.

  Update the docstring (the block at L512–L541 of WeilDivisor.lean) to
  match the equation form:
  - Remove the existential / "exists a non-zero element `t ∈ K`"
    framing.
  - State the equation `deg(positivePart(principal(algebraMap K K(C)
    t))) = [K(C) : K]` directly for any chosen `t` and `halg`, with a
    `% NOTE:` that the canonical witness in the project is
    `(localParameterAtInfty kbar).val`.
  - Body recipe remains unchanged (`Ideal.sum_ramification_inertia` +
    `Ideal.finrank_quotient_map` per `analogies/ratcurveiso-pin2.md`
    Decision 2).

### File: `AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RationalCurveIso.lean`

Two changes.

#### Change A — DELETE the file-local `WeilDivisor.positivePart` def (L416–L418)

The file-local

```
private noncomputable def WeilDivisor.positivePart {X : Scheme.{u}}
    (D : X.WeilDivisor) : X.WeilDivisor :=
  Finsupp.mapRange (fun n : ℤ => n ⊔ 0) (by simp) D
```

becomes redundant once the public `Scheme.WeilDivisor.positivePart`
in WeilDivisor.lean is imported. Delete L416–L418 (the def) and its
docstring block (L405–L415).

Any references in the file to `WeilDivisor.positivePart` (e.g. inside
`Hom.poleDivisor`'s body) will now resolve to
`AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.positivePart` from the
WeilDivisor.lean import — this is the desired outcome and is automatic
(no edit needed at the consumer sites; just ensure the imports +
namespace open are intact).

#### Change B — DELETE the file-local pin
`WeilDivisor.degree_positivePart_principal_localParameterAtInfty_eq_finrank`
(L444–L469)

The file-local

```
private theorem
    WeilDivisor.degree_positivePart_principal_localParameterAtInfty_eq_finrank
    ... :
    Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree
        (WeilDivisor.positivePart
          (Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal
            (algebraMap (ProjectiveLineBar kbar).left.functionField
              C.left.functionField (localParameterAtInfty kbar).val)
            halg)) =
      (Module.finrank
        (ProjectiveLineBar kbar).left.functionField
        C.left.functionField : ℤ) := by
  sorry
```

becomes the public
`Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank` (in
its reshaped equation form, see WeilDivisor.lean change above)
specialised at:
- `K = (ProjectiveLineBar kbar).left.functionField`
- `t = (localParameterAtInfty kbar).val`
- `halg` = the local `halg` from `Hom.poleDivisor`.

Delete L444–L469 (the pin) and its docstring block (L420–L443).

#### Change C — Update `Hom.poleDivisor_degree_eq_finrank` body (L585–L624)

Currently:

```
unfold Scheme.Hom.poleDivisor
exact
  WeilDivisor.degree_positivePart_principal_localParameterAtInfty_eq_finrank
    φ _hφ_non_const _
```

Update to:

```
unfold Scheme.Hom.poleDivisor
-- Apply the public WeilDivisor pin (equation form, specialised at
-- t = localParameterAtInfty kbar).
exact Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank
    C (localParameterAtInfty kbar).val _
```

where the `_` argument is Lean's elaboration of the local `halg` from
the `unfold`ed `Hom.poleDivisor` body.

NOTE: the public pin's `[Module.Finite K C.left.functionField]`
instance must be satisfiable at this call site. Since the
`Hom.poleDivisor_degree_eq_finrank` signature already requires
`[GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom]` + `[SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1
C.hom]` + non-constancy, the implied finite extension `K(ℙ¹) → K(C)`
should give the instance via some derivation. If the instance cannot
be derived, add `[Module.Finite (ProjectiveLineBar kbar).left.functionField
C.left.functionField]` as an explicit binder on
`Hom.poleDivisor_degree_eq_finrank` (matching what was implicit in the
file-local pin's signature).

## Affected Files

- `AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/WeilDivisor.lean` — signature
  reshape (~10 LOC change in the theorem signature + docstring update
  ~20 LOC).
- `AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RationalCurveIso.lean` — three
  cumulative edits: delete file-local def (~14 LOC removed incl.
  docstring), delete file-local pin (~50 LOC removed incl.
  docstring), update `Hom.poleDivisor_degree_eq_finrank` body
  (~5 LOC change).
- No other files should be affected (only `AlgebraicJacobian.lean`
  imports RationalCurveIso.lean as the umbrella, and the umbrella
  doesn't reference these symbols directly).

## Expected Outcome

After this refactor:

1. `lake build AlgebraicJacobian` GREEN (integration restored).
2. `WeilDivisor.lean` sorry count: **unchanged** at 3 (the existential
   pin → equation pin is still one typed-sorry; just reshaped).
3. `RationalCurveIso.lean` sorry count: **decreases by 1** (the
   file-local pin removed; `Hom.poleDivisor_degree_eq_finrank` body
   consumes the public pin axiom-clean modulo the public sorryAx).
4. Project axioms: `{propext, Classical.choice, Quot.sound}` for the
   non-sorry-consuming subset; `sorryAx` only via the SINGLE public
   pin `Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank`.
   The 10-consecutive-zero-axiom-build streak resumes the next time
   prover phase doesn't add a new typed sorry.
5. `\lean{...}` cross-refs in `blueprint/src/chapters/RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor.tex`
   `lem:degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank` and
   `RiemannRoch_RationalCurveIso.tex` `def:Hom.poleDivisor` continue
   to resolve correctly.

## What you must NOT do

- Do NOT attempt to PROVE the body of
  `degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank` — leave it as `sorry`
  (typed-sorry pin; body iter-191+ via Hartshorne II.6.9 chain). The
  refactor is signature reshape only.
- Do NOT touch other files (`AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean`,
  `OCofP.lean`, etc.) — the clash is localised to the two files
  above.
- Do NOT add or remove `\leanok` markers — those are managed by
  `sync_leanok` between prover and review.

## Verification at end of refactor

- `lake build AlgebraicJacobian` GREEN.
- `lean_diagnostic_messages` on both files reports only `sorry`
  warnings (no errors).
- `lean_verify AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank`
  reports `sorryAx` + standard kernel axioms.
- `lean_verify AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Hom.poleDivisor_degree_eq_finrank`
  reports `sorryAx` (via the public pin) + standard kernel axioms.
- File sorry counts as predicted above.

Concretely, \((D)_0\) is obtained from \(D\) by replacing each coefficient
  \(n_Y\) with \(\max(n_Y, 0)\). Equivalently, in the \(\Finsupp{\mathrm{PrimeDivisor}(X)}{\mathbb Z}\)
  presentation, the positive part is obtained either by the pointwise lattice
  join with the zero divisor — \(\,(D)_0 = D \sqcup 0\,\) — or, equivalently
  and more transparently for typeclass synthesis, by mapping the coefficients
  through the zero-preserving function \(n \mapsto n \sqcup 0\):
  \(\,(D)_0 = \mathtt{Finsupp.mapRange}\;(n \mapsto n \sqcup 0)\;D\). The two
  forms agree by the universal property of \(\mathtt{Finsupp.mapRange}\). The
  Lean implementation uses the \(\mathtt{mapRange}\) form to avoid the
